title: Lesson 39 — Scale

words.example: The dam was a colossal feat of engineering.

words.definition: enormous in size, quantity, or degree
words.example: A gargantuan buffet stretched the length of the hall.

words.definition: remarkably or impressively great in extent, size, or degree
words.example: The young violinist showed prodigious talent.

words.definition: extremely large or great, especially in scale
words.example: An immense crowd gathered for the eclipse.
A diminutive robot slipped through the narrow pipe.
Only a minuscule chance remained of finding survivors.

words.definition: so small or unimportant as to be not worth considering
words.example: The price difference was negligible over a year.
An infinitesimal error compounded over a million calculations.

words.definition: unreasonably or excessively large, especially of a price
words.example: Hotel prices near the stadium turned exorbitant that weekend.

words.definition: exceeding what is reasonable or necessary; lavishly wasteful
words.example: An extravagant wedding that emptied the family's savings.
He spent an inordinate amount of time on the footnotes.

words.definition: more than is necessary, normal, or desirable
words.example: The fine seemed excessive for such a small offense.

story: Building the observatory required a [[colossal|colossal]], almost [[gargantuan|gargantuan]] mirror, ground with [[prodigious|prodigious]] care and set inside an [[immense|immense]] dome. Beside it, the guidance chip looked absurdly [[diminutive|diminutive]] — a [[minuscule|minuscule]] wafer whose weight was practically [[negligible|negligible]], its margin of error [[infinitesimal|infinitesimal]]. The budget told a different story: the mirror's cost was [[exorbitant|exorbitant]], the opening gala [[extravagant|extravagant]], and one senator complained of an [[inordinate|inordinate]], frankly [[excessive|excessive]] amount spent on a single lens.
